title: Cholesteatoma
oneLiner: Destructive keratinising squamous epithelial cyst in the middle ear/mastoid; causes ossicular erosion, hearing loss, and intracranial spread if untreated. Requires surgery.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old man presents to the ENT clinic with a 6-month history of persistent, painless, purulent, foul-smelling discharge from the left ear. He reports progressive hearing loss on that side, and his wife says he sometimes mishears her. On examination, the external auditory canal is filled with greasy, white debris and an attic perforation is visible. A blue‑layered CT temporal bone shows a soft‑tissue mass eroding the scutum and ossicles.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Painless discharge** → unlike acute otitis media (painful) or mastoiditis (tender mastoid). Cholesteatoma is a chronic, indolent process.
- **Foul-smelling, purulent discharge** → keratin debris + secondary infection with anaerobes.
- **Progressive conductive hearing loss** → ossicular chain erosion (incus most commonly).
- **Greasy, white debris in ear canal** → keratin flakes; pathognomonic when seen.
- **Attic (pars flaccida) perforation** → typical for acquired cholesteatoma; the retraction pocket originates here.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent cholesteatoma** – no discharge, no pain; only hearing loss or a chance finding on CT for other reasons. Patient may have no otorrhoea, but a retraction pocket with keratin can still erode the facial nerve or labyrinth.
2. **Facial nerve palsy as first sign** – erosion of the tympanic segment of the facial nerve. A junior doctor might diagnose Bell’s palsy, but always check the ear in any facial palsy.
3. **Vertigo + hearing loss** – erosion of the lateral semicircular canal creates a labyrinthine fistula. Presents like serous labyrinthitis or Meniere’s, but the ear discharge and CT changes give it away.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Cholesteatoma is a benign but destructive growth of keratinising squamous epithelium that invades the middle ear and mastoid. It’s a “skin cyst in the wrong place” that expands by producing desquamated keratin, which becomes infected. It is **not a tumour**, but it behaves like one locally — eroding bone, ossicles, and adjacent structures.

**Why it matters:**
- **Common and often missed** – affects ~9 per 100,000 adults per year. Delayed diagnosis leads to irreversible hearing loss, facial nerve paralysis, labyrinthine fistula, and life‑threatening intracranial complications (meningitis, brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis).
- **Surgical disease** – medical treatment (antibiotic drops) only controls infection; definitive treatment is mastoidectomy + cholesteatoma excision.
- **High recurrence** – even after “complete” removal, residual disease in difficult-to-reach pockets or matrix fragments can re-form. Lifelong follow-up is required.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**

Cholesteatoma begins as a **retraction pocket** of the tympanic membrane. In 90% of acquired cases, it originates in the pars flaccida (attic) or the posterosuperior quadrant of the pars tensa. Eustachian tube dysfunction (e.g. from childhood otitis media, cleft palate, or allergic rhinitis) creates chronic negative pressure in the middle ear. This negative pressure pulls the TM inward, forming a pocket that traps desquamated keratin.

The trapped keratin becomes a nidus for infection (anaerobic bacteria – Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus). The expanding mass produces **collagenase, osteoclast‑activating factors, and pro‑inflammatory cytokines** (IL‑1, TNF‑α) that erode bone. Because the middle ear cavity is bounded by bone — the tegmen tympani, the lateral semicircular canal, and the facial nerve canal — the cholesteatoma follows the path of least resistance: through bone.

**Key signs and their mechanistic link:**
- **Foul-smelling discharge** → anaerobic bacteria + keratin debris in stagnant, hypoxic environment.
- **Conductive hearing loss** → ossicular chain erosion (incus most vulnerable; then malleus and stapes) + tympanic membrane perforation.
- **Attic or posterosuperior perforation** → the location of the retraction pocket.
- **White keratin debris** → desquamated squamous cells.
- **Vertigo and nystagmus** → erosion of the lateral semicircular canal (fistula sign – vertigo on tragal pressure or pneumatic otoscopy). This occurs when the cholesteatoma matrix lies directly over the bony canal.
- **Facial palsy** → erosion of the tympanic segment of the facial nerve (dehiscent nerve or direct compression + inflammation).

**Connection to pattern recognition:** the “painless, progressive hearing loss” is due to the silent bone‑eroding nature; the “foul discharge” is from the infected keratin; and the “attic perforation” is the visible portal.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Spontaneous retraction pocket development in a previously normal ear. | “No history of ear surgery or trauma; found on routine exam.” |
| **Congenital** | Epithelial rest from the first branchial arch remnant (within middle ear, behind intact TM). | “Child with an intact tympanic membrane and a white pearly mass behind it.” |
| **Acquired – post‑infectious** | Recurrent acute otitis media or chronic suppurative otitis media leading to TM retraction. | “History of multiple ear infections as a child; now has attic perforation.” |
| **Acquired – post‑surgical** | Residual or recurrent cholesteatoma after mastoidectomy (incomplete matrix removal or new retraction). | “Had cholesteatoma surgery 5 years ago; now presents with discharge again.” |
| **Acquired – trauma** | Iatrogenic introduction of squamous epithelium (e.g. tympanostomy tube insertion, foreign body). | “Ear discharge started after grommet insertion; CT shows soft tissue.” |
| **Eustachian tube dysfunction** | Cleft palate, down syndrome, allergic rhinitis – any cause of chronic negative middle ear pressure. | “Patient with cleft palate repair – now has hearing loss and retraction pocket.” |

**Most common cause:** Acquired cholesteatoma secondary to chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction → retraction pocket → keratin accumulation → infection.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis is clinical (otoscopy suggestive) plus CT confirmation. The “gold standard” is surgical findings at mastoidectomy.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Otoscopy (with microscope/pneumatic otoscopy)** | Attic or posterosuperior retraction pocket, greasy white debris, foul discharge, possible granulation tissue. | First‑line; high sensitivity in experienced hands. | **Single finding that confirms:** white keratin debris in a retraction pocket or attic perforation. |
| **Puretic (double‑layered) CT temporal bone (high‑resolution, non‑contrast)** | Soft‑tissue mass eroding the scutum, ossicles, tegmen, lateral semicircular canal; may show displacement of the chain. | Surgical planning – assess extension into mastoid, middle ear, epitympanum, and labyrinth. | **Single finding that rules in:** erosion of the scutum or ossicles by a soft‑tissue density. **Rule out:** smooth‑walled middle ear effusion (no erosion). |
| **MRI with diffusion‑weighted imaging (DWI)** | On DWI, cholesteatoma shows restricted diffusion (high signal) – differentiates it from granulation tissue or fluid (no restriction). | Useful for surgery follow‑up to detect residual disease (especially non‑echo planar DWI). Also for intracranial extension. | **High signal on DWI + no contrast enhancement** = cholesteatoma; if contrast enhances, think tumour or infection. |
| **Pure tone audiometry** | Conductive hearing loss (air‑bone gap) – typically moderate (30–50 dB). | Baseline for hearing; determines type and degree of loss. | **Air‑bone gap > 20 dB** suggests ossicular erosion. |
| **Microbiology of ear swab** | Anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus), Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus, Proteus. | Guides antibiotic choice if surgery is delayed or acute infection present. | Do not treat just to “sterilise” the cholesteatoma – only if spreading infection. |

**Pattern‑recognition:** On CT, the combination of an **eroded scutum** + a soft‑tissue opacification in the **Prussak space** (attic) is pathognomonic for pars flaccida cholesteatoma.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):** None – this is not an emergency unless there are signs of intracranial complication (meningism, focal neurology, fever, headache). In that case, urgent CT/MRI, IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole), and neurosurgical referral.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – pre‑surgical optimisation:**
- **If active infection:** prescribe topical antibiotic drops (e.g. ciprofloxacin‑dexamethasone otic suspension, 3–4 drops twice daily for 7–10 days). Do **not** use aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, tobramycin) if a labyrinthine fistula is suspected – they cause ototoxicity.
- **Dry ear cleaning:** suction clearance under microscope by ENT.
- **CT temporal bone** (if not done as outpatient).
- **Audiometry** (if not done).
- **Arrange surgery** – typically within weeks, not days unless complications.

**Long‑term:**
- **Definitive surgery:** **Canal wall up (CWU) mastoidectomy** vs. **canal wall down (CWD) mastoidectomy**. Choice depends on disease extent, size of meatus, and surgeon preference.
    - CWU: preserved posterior canal wall, better hearing outcome (ossiculoplasty possible), but higher recurrence.
    - CWD: exteriorise the mastoid cavity – lower recurrence, but cavity needs lifelong care (keeping clean, avoiding water). Better for extensive disease.
- **Ossiculoplasty** (if ossicular chain eroded) – partial ossicular replacement prosthesis (PORP) or total ossicular replacement prosthesis (TORP).
- **Second‑look surgery** – often planned 6–12 months later to re‑inspect for residual disease (especially after CWU).
- **Long‑term follow‑up:** clinical otoscopy every 6–12 months; imaging (DWI MRI) if suspicious.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ **Do not prescribe oral antibiotics alone** – they don’t reach the avascular keratin mass adequately.
- ❌ **Do not delay surgery** for a “trial of medical treatment” – this can cause progression of bone erosion.
- ❌ **Do not perform a total ossicular replacement (TORP) in a wet ear** – failure rate high.
- ❌ **Do not assume a “dry” retraction pocket is safe** – it can still erode the facial nerve.

**Drug doses inline:**
- **Ciprofloxacin 0.3% + dexamethasone 0.1% otic suspension** – 4 drops twice daily for 7–10 days.
- **Ceftriaxone IV 2 g once daily + metronidazole IV 500 mg three times daily** – if intracranial complication suspected.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Cholesteatoma | Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM) | Middle Ear Effusion (OME) | Glomus Tympanicum |
|---------|---------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually painless | Dull ache or feeling of pressure | None | None (pulsatile tinnitus) |
| **Discharge** | Foul‑smelling, purulent, greasy keratin | Purulent but not foul; may be mucoid | None (unless perforation) | None |
| **Otoscopy** | Attic/posterosuperior retraction + white debris | Central perforation (pars tensa) with mucosal oedema | Retracted TM with amber fluid, no erosion | Red, pulsatile mass behind intact TM |
| **CT temporal bone** | Scutum erosion, ossicular erosion, soft‑tissue mass with bony destruction | Central perforation, mucosal thickening, no bone erosion | Fluid‑filled middle ear, normal ossicles | Homogeneous enhancing mass in middle ear (erodes jugular bulb) |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive, progressive | Conductive, usually stable | Conductive (mild‑moderate) | Conductive or sensorineural if large |
| **Key differentiator** | White keratin debris + bone erosion | Central perforation + no debris | Fluid levels, no keratin | Pulsatile tinnitus + contrast enhancement |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Facial nerve palsy** | Sudden or gradual facial asymmetry, inability to close eye, drooping mouth. | Can be painless; no herpetic rash (vs Ramsey Hunt). | Urgent CT/MRI; surgical decompression. Start high‑dose steroids (prednisolone 60 mg daily for 7 days) if suspected inflammatory component. |
| **Labyrinthine fistula** | Vertigo, nystagmus (often horizontal), high‑frequency sensorineural hearing loss. | **Fistula sign** – vertigo on tragal pressure or pneumatic otoscopy. | Urgent CT to confirm; surgical repair (seal the fistula with fascia). Avoid aminoglycoside drops. |
| **Brain abscess** | Headache, fever, focal neurological deficit, seizures, GCS drop. | CT head shows ring‑enhancing lesion; lumbar puncture is contraindicated. | Emergent neurosurgical drainage + IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole + vancomycin). |
| **Meningitis** | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, headache. | Kernig’s sign, Brudzinski’s sign, CSF pleocytosis. | IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole + dexamethasone; urgent CT to rule out abscess. |
| **Sigmoid sinus thrombosis** | Headache, fever, otalgia, papilloedema, signs of raised ICP. | **Griesinger’s sign** – oedema over mastoid process + pain on rotation of head. | MRV/CTV; start anticoagulation (heparin) + IV antibiotics; need for surgical drainage of mastoid. |
| **Petrous apicitis** | Deep ear pain, abducens nerve palsy (CN VI), Gradenigo syndrome (CN VI + CN V1 distribution pain). | Diplopia; CT shows erosion of petrous apex. | IV antibiotics for 6 weeks; surgical drainage (petrosectomy) may be needed. |

**Late‑presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):** A patient with a known history of ear discharge and hearing loss presents with acute headache, fever, and a third nerve palsy — suspect a temporal lobe or cerebellar abscess secondary to cholesteatoma.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: “CHOLESTEATOMA” for complications**
- C – Cerebellar abscess
- H – Hearing loss (conductive then sensorineural)
- O – Otogenic meningitis
- L – Labyrinthine fistula
- E – Extradural abscess
- S – Sigmoid sinus thrombosis
- T – Tympanic‑facial nerve palsy
- E – Erosion of ossicles
- A – Attic retraction pocket
- T – Tympanic membrane perforation
- O – Ossicular discontinuity
- M – Mastoiditis
- A – Adhesive otitis media (later stage)

**Mnemonic: “CRUDE” for management steps**
- C – CT temporal bone
- R – Refer to ENT
- U – Urgent surgery if complications
- D – Drops (ciprofloxacin‑dexamethasone) if infection
- E – Excision (mastoidectomy) + follow‑up

**High‑yield clinical pearls:**
1. **Any unilateral conductive hearing loss in a young adult** – think cholesteatoma until proven otherwise.
2. **The attic perforation is a “red flag” for cholesteatoma** – never dismiss as simple perforation.
3. **Facial palsy + ear discharge = cholesteatoma until proven** – do not label as Bell’s palsy.
4. **Pneumatic otoscopy** can reveal the fistula sign – but be gentle; sudden vertigo may cause injury.
5. **CT without contrast is enough for diagnosis** – contrast only needed if intracranial extension suspected.
6. **Recurrent cholesteatoma is common** – even after “complete” resection, 10–30% recur. Follow‑up is lifelong.
7. **Never use aminoglycoside drops if a fistula is possible** – they are ototoxic and could cause permanent hearing loss.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- ✗ Diagnosing “otitis externa” when the discharge is coming through an attic perforation.
- ✗ Prescribing oral antibiotics for a month without ear toilet.
- ✗ Ordering a CT with contrast as the first test – it adds radiation and masks bony details; non‑contrast high‑resolution is best.
- ✗ Doing an ossiculoplasty in the same setting as cholesteatoma removal (high risk of prosthetic infection) – staged approach preferred.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call ENT senior (registrar/consultant):**
- Suspicion of intracranial complication (fever, headache, neck stiffness, focal neurology, seizures).
- Facial nerve palsy (sudden or rapid onset) – possible need for urgent decompression.
- Labyrinthine fistula signs (vertigo + hearing loss) – urgent CT and surgical planning.
- Uncontrolled infection despite topical therapy – possible need for IV antibiotics or surgical drainage.

**When to call the crash team / ICU:**
- GCS < 13
- Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, Cushing’s response – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respirations).
- Seizures.
- Suspected brain abscess with midline shift.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – escalate to senior; if ≥ 7, consider sepsis protocol.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** – concern for sepsis (especially if meningitis or sinus thrombosis).
- **GCS < 12** – consider intubation for airway protection if causing brainstem compression.
- **Serum sodium** – hyponatraemia can accompany meningitis (SIADH).

**Take‑home message:** Cholesteatoma is a silent destroyer. The junior doctor’s role is to recognise the classic trio (painless foul discharge + conductive hearing loss + attic perforation) and act quickly to refer for surgery and permanent follow‑up. Never miss it – the consequences can be devastating.

## Cholesteatoma

**One-liner:** Destructive keratinising squamous epithelial cyst in the middle ear/mastoid; causes ossicular erosion, hearing loss, and intracranial spread if untreated. Requires surgery.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old man presents to the ENT clinic with a 6-month history of persistent, painless, purulent, foul-smelling discharge from the left ear. He reports progressive hearing loss on that side, and his wife says he sometimes mishears her. On examination, the external auditory canal is filled with greasy, white debris and an attic perforation is visible. A blue‑layered CT temporal bone shows a soft‑tissue mass eroding the scutum and ossicles.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Painless discharge** → unlike acute otitis media (painful) or mastoiditis (tender mastoid). Cholesteatoma is a chronic, indolent process.
- **Foul-smelling, purulent discharge** → keratin debris + secondary infection with anaerobes.
- **Progressive conductive hearing loss** → ossicular chain erosion (incus most commonly).
- **Greasy, white debris in ear canal** → keratin flakes; pathognomonic when seen.
- **Attic (pars flaccida) perforation** → typical for acquired cholesteatoma; the retraction pocket originates here.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent cholesteatoma** – no discharge, no pain; only hearing loss or a chance finding on CT for other reasons. Patient may have no otorrhoea, but a retraction pocket with keratin can still erode the facial nerve or labyrinth.
2. **Facial nerve palsy as first sign** – erosion of the tympanic segment of the facial nerve. A junior doctor might diagnose Bell’s palsy, but always check the ear in any facial palsy.
3. **Vertigo + hearing loss** – erosion of the lateral semicircular canal creates a labyrinthine fistula. Presents like serous labyrinthitis or Meniere’s, but the ear discharge and CT changes give it away.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Cholesteatoma is a benign but destructive growth of keratinising squamous epithelium that invades the middle ear and mastoid. It’s a “skin cyst in the wrong place” that expands by producing desquamated keratin, which becomes infected. It is **not a tumour**, but it behaves like one locally — eroding bone, ossicles, and adjacent structures.

**Why it matters:**
- **Common and often missed** – affects ~9 per 100,000 adults per year. Delayed diagnosis leads to irreversible hearing loss, facial nerve paralysis, labyrinthine fistula, and life‑threatening intracranial complications (meningitis, brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis).
- **Surgical disease** – medical treatment (antibiotic drops) only controls infection; definitive treatment is mastoidectomy + cholesteatoma excision.
- **High recurrence** – even after “complete” removal, residual disease in difficult-to-reach pockets or matrix fragments can re-form. Lifelong follow-up is required.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**

Cholesteatoma begins as a **retraction pocket** of the tympanic membrane. In 90% of acquired cases, it originates in the pars flaccida (attic) or the posterosuperior quadrant of the pars tensa. Eustachian tube dysfunction (e.g. from childhood otitis media, cleft palate, or allergic rhinitis) creates chronic negative pressure in the middle ear. This negative pressure pulls the TM inward, forming a pocket that traps desquamated keratin.

The trapped keratin becomes a nidus for infection (anaerobic bacteria – Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus). The expanding mass produces **collagenase, osteoclast‑activating factors, and pro‑inflammatory cytokines** (IL‑1, TNF‑α) that erode bone. Because the middle ear cavity is bounded by bone — the tegmen tympani, the lateral semicircular canal, and the facial nerve canal — the cholesteatoma follows the path of least resistance: through bone.

**Key signs and their mechanistic link:**
- **Foul-smelling discharge** → anaerobic bacteria + keratin debris in stagnant, hypoxic environment.
- **Conductive hearing loss** → ossicular chain erosion (incus most vulnerable; then malleus and stapes) + tympanic membrane perforation.
- **Attic or posterosuperior perforation** → the location of the retraction pocket.
- **White keratin debris** → desquamated squamous cells.
- **Vertigo and nystagmus** → erosion of the lateral semicircular canal (fistula sign – vertigo on tragal pressure or pneumatic otoscopy). This occurs when the cholesteatoma matrix lies directly over the bony canal.
- **Facial palsy** → erosion of the tympanic segment of the facial nerve (dehiscent nerve or direct compression + inflammation).

**Connection to pattern recognition:** the “painless, progressive hearing loss” is due to the silent bone‑eroding nature; the “foul discharge” is from the infected keratin; and the “attic perforation” is the visible portal.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Spontaneous retraction pocket development in a previously normal ear. | “No history of ear surgery or trauma; found on routine exam.” |
| **Congenital** | Epithelial rest from the first branchial arch remnant (within middle ear, behind intact TM). | “Child with an intact tympanic membrane and a white pearly mass behind it.” |
| **Acquired – post‑infectious** | Recurrent acute otitis media or chronic suppurative otitis media leading to TM retraction. | “History of multiple ear infections as a child; now has attic perforation.” |
| **Acquired – post‑surgical** | Residual or recurrent cholesteatoma after mastoidectomy (incomplete matrix removal or new retraction). | “Had cholesteatoma surgery 5 years ago; now presents with discharge again.” |
| **Acquired – trauma** | Iatrogenic introduction of squamous epithelium (e.g. tympanostomy tube insertion, foreign body). | “Ear discharge started after grommet insertion; CT shows soft tissue.” |
| **Eustachian tube dysfunction** | Cleft palate, down syndrome, allergic rhinitis – any cause of chronic negative middle ear pressure. | “Patient with cleft palate repair – now has hearing loss and retraction pocket.” |

**Most common cause:** Acquired cholesteatoma secondary to chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction → retraction pocket → keratin accumulation → infection.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system; diagnosis is clinical (otoscopy suggestive) plus CT confirmation. The “gold standard” is surgical findings at mastoidectomy.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Otoscopy (with microscope/pneumatic otoscopy)** | Attic or posterosuperior retraction pocket, greasy white debris, foul discharge, possible granulation tissue. | First‑line; high sensitivity in experienced hands. | **Single finding that confirms:** white keratin debris in a retraction pocket or attic perforation. |
| **Puretic (double‑layered) CT temporal bone (high‑resolution, non‑contrast)** | Soft‑tissue mass eroding the scutum, ossicles, tegmen, lateral semicircular canal; may show displacement of the chain. | Surgical planning – assess extension into mastoid, middle ear, epitympanum, and labyrinth. | **Single finding that rules in:** erosion of the scutum or ossicles by a soft‑tissue density. **Rule out:** smooth‑walled middle ear effusion (no erosion). |
| **MRI with diffusion‑weighted imaging (DWI)** | On DWI, cholesteatoma shows restricted diffusion (high signal) – differentiates it from granulation tissue or fluid (no restriction). | Useful for surgery follow‑up to detect residual disease (especially non‑echo planar DWI). Also for intracranial extension. | **High signal on DWI + no contrast enhancement** = cholesteatoma; if contrast enhances, think tumour or infection. |
| **Pure tone audiometry** | Conductive hearing loss (air‑bone gap) – typically moderate (30–50 dB). | Baseline for hearing; determines type and degree of loss. | **Air‑bone gap > 20 dB** suggests ossicular erosion. |
| **Microbiology of ear swab** | Anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus), Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus, Proteus. | Guides antibiotic choice if surgery is delayed or acute infection present. | Do not treat just to “sterilise” the cholesteatoma – only if spreading infection. |

**Pattern‑recognition:** On CT, the combination of an **eroded scutum** + a soft‑tissue opacification in the **Prussak space** (attic) is pathognomonic for pars flaccida cholesteatoma.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):** None – this is not an emergency unless there are signs of intracranial complication (meningism, focal neurology, fever, headache). In that case, urgent CT/MRI, IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole), and neurosurgical referral.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – pre‑surgical optimisation:**
- **If active infection:** prescribe topical antibiotic drops (e.g. ciprofloxacin‑dexamethasone otic suspension, 3–4 drops twice daily for 7–10 days). Do **not** use aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, tobramycin) if a labyrinthine fistula is suspected – they cause ototoxicity.
- **Dry ear cleaning:** suction clearance under microscope by ENT.
- **CT temporal bone** (if not done as outpatient).
- **Audiometry** (if not done).
- **Arrange surgery** – typically within weeks, not days unless complications.

**Long‑term:**
- **Definitive surgery:** **Canal wall up (CWU) mastoidectomy** vs. **canal wall down (CWD) mastoidectomy**. Choice depends on disease extent, size of meatus, and surgeon preference.
    - CWU: preserved posterior canal wall, better hearing outcome (ossiculoplasty possible), but higher recurrence.
    - CWD: exteriorise the mastoid cavity – lower recurrence, but cavity needs lifelong care (keeping clean, avoiding water). Better for extensive disease.
- **Ossiculoplasty** (if ossicular chain eroded) – partial ossicular replacement prosthesis (PORP) or total ossicular replacement prosthesis (TORP).
- **Second‑look surgery** – often planned 6–12 months later to re‑inspect for residual disease (especially after CWU).
- **Long‑term follow‑up:** clinical otoscopy every 6–12 months; imaging (DWI MRI) if suspicious.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ **Do not prescribe oral antibiotics alone** – they don’t reach the avascular keratin mass adequately.
- ❌ **Do not delay surgery** for a “trial of medical treatment” – this can cause progression of bone erosion.
- ❌ **Do not perform a total ossicular replacement (TORP) in a wet ear** – failure rate high.
- ❌ **Do not assume a “dry” retraction pocket is safe** – it can still erode the facial nerve.

**Drug doses inline:**
- **Ciprofloxacin 0.3% + dexamethasone 0.1% otic suspension** – 4 drops twice daily for 7–10 days.
- **Ceftriaxone IV 2 g once daily + metronidazole IV 500 mg three times daily** – if intracranial complication suspected.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Cholesteatoma | Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM) | Middle Ear Effusion (OME) | Glomus Tympanicum |
|---------|---------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually painless | Dull ache or feeling of pressure | None | None (pulsatile tinnitus) |
| **Discharge** | Foul‑smelling, purulent, greasy keratin | Purulent but not foul; may be mucoid | None (unless perforation) | None |
| **Otoscopy** | Attic/posterosuperior retraction + white debris | Central perforation (pars tensa) with mucosal oedema | Retracted TM with amber fluid, no erosion | Red, pulsatile mass behind intact TM |
| **CT temporal bone** | Scutum erosion, ossicular erosion, soft‑tissue mass with bony destruction | Central perforation, mucosal thickening, no bone erosion | Fluid‑filled middle ear, normal ossicles | Homogeneous enhancing mass in middle ear (erodes jugular bulb) |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive, progressive | Conductive, usually stable | Conductive (mild‑moderate) | Conductive or sensorineural if large |
| **Key differentiator** | White keratin debris + bone erosion | Central perforation + no debris | Fluid levels, no keratin | Pulsatile tinnitus + contrast enhancement |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Facial nerve palsy** | Sudden or gradual facial asymmetry, inability to close eye, drooping mouth. | Can be painless; no herpetic rash (vs Ramsey Hunt). | Urgent CT/MRI; surgical decompression. Start high‑dose steroids (prednisolone 60 mg daily for 7 days) if suspected inflammatory component. |
| **Labyrinthine fistula** | Vertigo, nystagmus (often horizontal), high‑frequency sensorineural hearing loss. | **Fistula sign** – vertigo on tragal pressure or pneumatic otoscopy. | Urgent CT to confirm; surgical repair (seal the fistula with fascia). Avoid aminoglycoside drops. |
| **Brain abscess** | Headache, fever, focal neurological deficit, seizures, GCS drop. | CT head shows ring‑enhancing lesion; lumbar puncture is contraindicated. | Emergent neurosurgical drainage + IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole + vancomycin). |
| **Meningitis** | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, headache. | Kernig’s sign, Brudzinski’s sign, CSF pleocytosis. | IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole + dexamethasone; urgent CT to rule out abscess. |
| **Sigmoid sinus thrombosis** | Headache, fever, otalgia, papilloedema, signs of raised ICP. | **Griesinger’s sign** – oedema over mastoid process + pain on rotation of head. | MRV/CTV; start anticoagulation (heparin) + IV antibiotics; need for surgical drainage of mastoid. |
| **Petrous apicitis** | Deep ear pain, abducens nerve palsy (CN VI), Gradenigo syndrome (CN VI + CN V1 distribution pain). | Diplopia; CT shows erosion of petrous apex. | IV antibiotics for 6 weeks; surgical drainage (petrosectomy) may be needed. |

**Late‑presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):** A patient with a known history of ear discharge and hearing loss presents with acute headache, fever, and a third nerve palsy — suspect a temporal lobe or cerebellar abscess secondary to cholesteatoma.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: “CHOLESTEATOMA” for complications**
- C – Cerebellar abscess
- H – Hearing loss (conductive then sensorineural)
- O – Otogenic meningitis
- L – Labyrinthine fistula
- E – Extradural abscess
- S – Sigmoid sinus thrombosis
- T – Tympanic‑facial nerve palsy
- E – Erosion of ossicles
- A – Attic retraction pocket
- T – Tympanic membrane perforation
- O – Ossicular discontinuity
- M – Mastoiditis
- A – Adhesive otitis media (later stage)

**Mnemonic: “CRUDE” for management steps**
- C – CT temporal bone
- R – Refer to ENT
- U – Urgent surgery if complications
- D – Drops (ciprofloxacin‑dexamethasone) if infection
- E – Excision (mastoidectomy) + follow‑up

**High‑yield clinical pearls:**
1. **Any unilateral conductive hearing loss in a young adult** – think cholesteatoma until proven otherwise.
2. **The attic perforation is a “red flag” for cholesteatoma** – never dismiss as simple perforation.
3. **Facial palsy + ear discharge = cholesteatoma until proven** – do not label as Bell’s palsy.
4. **Pneumatic otoscopy** can reveal the fistula sign – but be gentle; sudden vertigo may cause injury.
5. **CT without contrast is enough for diagnosis** – contrast only needed if intracranial extension suspected.
6. **Recurrent cholesteatoma is common** – even after “complete” resection, 10–30% recur. Follow‑up is lifelong.
7. **Never use aminoglycoside drops if a fistula is possible** – they are ototoxic and could cause permanent hearing loss.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- ✗ Diagnosing “otitis externa” when the discharge is coming through an attic perforation.
- ✗ Prescribing oral antibiotics for a month without ear toilet.
- ✗ Ordering a CT with contrast as the first test – it adds radiation and masks bony details; non‑contrast high‑resolution is best.
- ✗ Doing an ossiculoplasty in the same setting as cholesteatoma removal (high risk of prosthetic infection) – staged approach preferred.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call ENT senior (registrar/consultant):**
- Suspicion of intracranial complication (fever, headache, neck stiffness, focal neurology, seizures).
- Facial nerve palsy (sudden or rapid onset) – possible need for urgent decompression.
- Labyrinthine fistula signs (vertigo + hearing loss) – urgent CT and surgical planning.
- Uncontrolled infection despite topical therapy – possible need for IV antibiotics or surgical drainage.

**When to call the crash team / ICU:**
- GCS < 13
- Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, Cushing’s response – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respirations).
- Seizures.
- Suspected brain abscess with midline shift.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – escalate to senior; if ≥ 7, consider sepsis protocol.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** – concern for sepsis (especially if meningitis or sinus thrombosis).
- **GCS < 12** – consider intubation for airway protection if causing brainstem compression.
- **Serum sodium** – hyponatraemia can accompany meningitis (SIADH).

**Take‑home message:** Cholesteatoma is a silent destroyer. The junior doctor’s role is to recognise the classic trio (painless foul discharge + conductive hearing loss + attic perforation) and act quickly to refer for surgery and permanent follow‑up. Never miss it – the consequences can be devastating.
